title: CLI Flags To Specify Custom Config File Path
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

Currently a number of environment variables are used to specify the custom config file paths (CONTAINERS_CONF, CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF, CONTAINERS_REGISTRIES_CONF) would it be possible to consolidate this into a CLI flag format? For example --config, --storage, --registry, etc. An alternative, potentially superior approach, could also be the addition of a --config-dir flag which directs podman to only recognize config files in the specified directory.

comments.body:
The problem with a cli option is that config files (at least containers.conf) are parsed before cli options.
This doesn't necessarily mean that it cannot be implemented but doing so would require big changes.

Is there a specific reason why using environment variables does not work for you?
We have tried this in the past and end up with a catch-22, which is why we use Environment variables.

The problem is Podman is using configuration files from 4 different projects.  containers/storage, containers/image, containers/common and Podman itself.  Not all of the configuration files are in the same directories and are handled the same way.  Your request would need to be in each of the subpackages, and could cause breaking changes. IE XDG_CONFIG_HOME is too generic. CONTAINERS_CONFIG_DIR, might be something we would consider, but you need to get maintainers of containers/image and Storage to agree.
